title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Lansing enacts a six-month moratorium on data centers to buy time for rules.

lines.text: Good evening, and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Tonight we're diving into Lansing's new six-month pause on data center proposals and what it signals for communities across the state.
Emmy, the Lansing City Council just voted today to block any new data center applications until January 2027.
That's a big step. Why now, and what exactly does this moratorium do?
It stops the city from even considering fresh proposals while officials work on permanent zoning and permitting rules. The goal is to get ahead of the rush before projects lock in.
How did we get here? What's the recent history that pushed the council to act?

Concerns have been building for months. Residents in several mid-Michigan towns have organized against noise, power strain, and farmland loss. Yesterday a group in Mason held a fundraiser to fight a proposed project, and on Saturday dozens rallied in Vicksburg over similar issues.
So Lansing is responding to that groundswell. What are the main worries driving these local fights?

Power demand is huge. One large facility can pull as much electricity as a small city, which means higher bills for everyone if utilities have to build new generation. Water use for cooling is another flashpoint, especially in areas already watching aquifer levels.
And farmland? A lot of these sites go on prime agricultural land.

Exactly. Once it's paved over for servers, it's gone for good. Towns also lose tax revenue control when projects get big abatements, like the one Manchester approved today after slashing the original ask by 89 percent.
Who are the key players pushing back versus those wanting the projects?

On one side you have residents, township boards, and groups like the one suing the Dowagiac facility over nonstop noise. On the other, utilities and developers argue the jobs and tax base are worth it, though even utility allies are now attacking vocal critics amid scrutiny over rising rates.
What happens next in Lansing? Do they have a timeline for those permanent rules?
The 182-day window gives them until early next year to draft siting standards, power caps, and noise protections. If they don't move fast, the pause could just delay the same fights elsewhere.
Governor Whitmer released a state growth plan yesterday that sets some of those standards. How does the Lansing move fit with that?

It lines up. The plan requires data centers to follow new siting, power, and water rules or lose state incentives. Local moratoriums like this one can buy time for towns to align with the state framework.
Is there any sign other cities will follow Lansing's lead?

Negaunee Township in Marquette County is already holding a public hearing August 13 on new zoning rules. That's a direct parallel. A handful of other communities have paused or tightened rules in recent weeks too.
What about the stalled state legislation? The House Speaker held up a bill with bipartisan support on Saturday.
That bill would have created statewide guardrails on siting and tax breaks. Without it, towns are left writing their own rules one by one, which is why these local pauses keep popping up.
Bottom line for listeners: does this moratorium actually protect residents or just kick the can down the road?
It buys breathing room to write better rules, but only if the council uses the time. Otherwise projects just shift to the next willing township.
Let's turn to some of the other stories from the last couple days.
Yesterday residents in Mason raised cash to fight a proposed data center, worried about noise and lost farmland right in their backyards.
That matches the pattern we're seeing statewide. How organized is the opposition getting?

Pretty organized. The Mason effort is one of several fundraisers and rallies. On Saturday Vicksburg saw a peaceful protest against planned projects in Kalamazoo County, and organizers say more actions are coming.
Switching to west Michigan, a utility is facing questions this week on power and water for the region's first major AI campus in Kent County.
Neighbors are already asking how the grid and local supply will handle the added load. The public hearing should give some answers.
And in southwest Michigan, Dowagiac residents filed a lawsuit on Sunday claiming nonstop noise from a hyperscale facility is ruining daily life.
The case highlights how some approvals happened without strong noise rules. It could set a precedent for other towns.
On the policy side, Whitmer's plan from yesterday ties incentives to compliance with new standards.
Companies that ignore the siting, power, and water guidelines risk losing state support. It's the first statewide attempt to steer growth.
Meanwhile in Manchester, the township granted a tax break today after the developer cut the original request by 89 percent.
Still less revenue for schools and services than first proposed, but the project moves forward with long-term fiscal impacts locked in.
And utility allies are going after a vocal data center critic today as scrutiny grows over power costs.
The attacks come right as residents worry bills will rise if projects keep getting approved without checks.
Now let's look ahead to meetings listeners can actually attend.
Mark your calendar for August 13. Negaunee Township in Marquette County holds a hearing on proposed data center zoning rules that will shape land use, power demand, and the local tax base.
That hearing could set the tone for how northern Michigan handles these projects.
Also this week, the utility serving Kent County will hold a public hearing on serving the new Microsoft AI campus. Neighbors will get a chance to ask about grid and water strain.
Those are the two big ones on the immediate horizon.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Lansing Hits Pause: 182-Day Moratorium on New Data Centers
